comments:
Summary:
This paper studies a non-numeric, symbolic model of episodic reinforcement learning in which transitions are nondeterministic and reward is a boolean flag on state-action pairs. The authors define an elementary learning procedure which: marks states visited during an episode, replaces the action for a state when the state is revisited in the same trial, and resets the visited flags between trials. They introduce a graph-theoretic notion reducibility (layers to goal states), prove that reducible tasks are learnable under a natural fairness assumption (all available choices explored infinitely often), and syntactically characterize the final policy of any converging run (forward set contained in backward set). The paper also proves simple necessary conditions and discusses the role of an “unstable” set. Examples and simulations (grid corridors, chain tasks) illustrate convergence behavior and empirical scaling.

Strengths:
- The paper gives a clean, self-contained symbolic formalization of a path-oriented view of reinforcement learning that avoids numeric reward/value functions. This is intellectually appealing and bridges RL with discrete verification-style reasoning.
- Main theoretical results are natural and mathematically clear: the reducibility sufficiency theorem and the forward ⊆ backward characterization of the final policy (useful in simulators) are both meaningful contributions.
- The fairness assumption is well-motivated and formalized; the paper explicitly demonstrates its necessity by counterexample.
- The manuscript discusses connections to biological plausibility (dopamine/eligibility traces) and prior RL convergence results, and includes illustrative simulations and implementation details.

Weaknesses and suggestions:
- The fairness assumption is central. While it is standard to require exploration, it would strengthen the paper to discuss more explicitly which practical exploration strategies satisfy the fairness requirement, or to provide probabilistic versions (e.g., with i.i.d. sampling, convergence with probability 1). The current model uses a deterministic round-robin scheme to argue existence of fair runs; a probabilistic treatment would increase practical relevance.
- The gap between the sufficient (reducibility) and necessary conditions is acknowledged but remains large. More analysis or structural characterizations of the unstable set and how practical algorithms can detect/avoid it (beyond informal discussion) would improve completeness.
- Time-to-convergence is only empirically explored; a theoretical discussion of bounds (or complexity of deciding reducibility) would be valuable. The paper notes a relation to dynamic programming but does not state complexity explicitly.
- The experimental section is useful but limited: choices (uniform sampling for nondeterminism and action selection) are reasonable but other distributions/noise models could be explored or at least discussed as limitations.
- Some proofs are lengthy/dense; readability could be improved by moving some technical lemmas to appendix and adding more intuition and examples in the main text.

Minor comments:
- Clarify how initial policies are chosen in practice and how sensitive convergence time is to that choice.
- Explicitly state complexity of computing reduce(T) and the forward/backward fixpoints.
- Briefly contrast with convergence proofs for tabular RL (e.g., Q-learning) in stochastic settings—what is gained/ lost by removing numeric values.

Overall assessment:
This is a thoughtful theoretical contribution that offers a novel non-numeric perspective on convergence for episodic navigation-like tasks. Results are sound and the exposition is mostly clear. With some modest extensions (probabilistic fairness, deeper treatment of unstable sets or time-to-convergence) the paper could have stronger impact. I recommend acceptance (strongly positive).
